summary: The q4 dataset contains 10 problems (q1-q10), each showing exactly 100 issues with no further differentiating metadata such as issue type, status breakdown, or timestamps. The uniform issue counts and absence of resolved/open distinctions make it impossible to reliably identify high-traction problems, solvability candidates, or proof completion status from the provided context. Additional granular data is required before actionable prioritization can be confidently performed.
problems: All 10 problems (q1-q10) report an identical 100 issues each, which suggests either a placeholder/default value or a data capture artifact rather than genuine activity differentiation.
No issue status breakdown (open vs. resolved) is provided, so proof progress and solvability cannot be assessed.
No metadata on issue type, severity, recency, or contributor activity is available to rank problems by traction.
The 'Proof Status' section duplicates raw issue counts rather than reporting actual proof states (e.g., proven, in-progress, blocked).
highlights: Complete coverage: all 10 problems in the dataset have associated issue records, indicating consistent tracking across the problem set.
Uniform structure provides a clean baseline for instrumenting richer metrics once status and type fields are added.

suggested_todos.title: Instrument issue tracking to capture open vs. resolved status per problem
Add issue metadata (type, severity, last-updated timestamp) to enable traction ranking
Verify whether the uniform '100 issues' value is real data or a placeholder/export bug
Define a concrete 'Proof Status' taxonomy (proven, in-progress, blocked, open) and populate it
Once enriched data is available, re-run analysis to identify near-solvable problems
Establish a weekly delta report to surface changes in issue counts and proof progress
